content.text: A standard Product Requirements Document (PRD) specifies what a product should do and how success is measured. For GenAI features, that foundation is necessary but insufficient. Probabilistic outputs, model versioning, safety controls, cost-per-inference, and evaluation datasets are all concerns that do not exist in conventional software — and none of them belong in a generic PRD template. This document specifies the eight sections a GenAI PRD must add to a standard template, what each contains, and who signs off before the sprint begins.

attrs.alt: GenAI PRD Section Map — Standard vs. GenAI-Specific Sections

content.text: The AI Capability Brief answers three questions: what AI task is being performed, which model or approach will perform it, and why is this approach feasible given the available data and timelines?

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class AICapabilityBrief:
    task_type:        str    # "text_generation" | "classification" | "extraction" | "retrieval" | "agentic"
    model_approach:   str    # specific model or architecture (e.g. "GPT-4o via API", "fine-tuned Llama-3.1-8B")
    input_modalities: list[str]   # ["text"] | ["text", "image"] | etc.
    output_type:      str    # "structured_json" | "natural_language" | "decision" | "ranked_list"
    feasibility_evidence: str   # prototype results, benchmarks, or prior art that justify feasibility
    known_limitations: list[str]  # explicit list of what the model cannot do reliably
    model_version_pinned: bool = True   # whether model version is fixed at release

    def sign_off_checklist(self) -> list[str]:
        checks = [
            f"Task type '{self.task_type}' confirmed feasible by ML engineer",
            f"Model '{self.model_approach}' approved for production use (licence, cost, latency)",
            f"Feasibility evidence reviewed: {self.feasibility_evidence[:60]}...",
        ]
        if not self.model_version_pinned:
            checks.append("WARNING: model version not pinned — eval results may not match production")
        return checks

EXAMPLE_BRIEF = AICapabilityBrief(
    task_type="extraction",
    model_approach="GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI API, model version 2024-05-13",
    input_modalities=["text"],
    output_type="structured_json",
    feasibility_evidence="Pilot on 50 contracts: 94% clause extraction accuracy (human eval, Oct 2024)",
    known_limitations=[
        "Accuracy drops to ~78% on contracts longer than 80 pages (context truncation)",
        "Does not reliably identify jurisdiction-specific clauses for non-UK law",
        "Handwritten amendments in scanned PDFs not supported",
    ],
    model_version_pinned=True,
)

field is the most important and most commonly omitted part of the Capability Brief. Omitting it does not make the limitations disappear — it ensures they are discovered in production instead of in the PRD review.
GenAI features require explicit performance thresholds before any code is written. Targets should be specified per metric, per user-facing scenario, and per persona segment where they differ.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class AccuracyTarget:
    metric_name:     str     # e.g. "clause_extraction_accuracy", "intent_classification_f1"
    threshold:       float   # minimum acceptable value (0-1)
    measurement_set: str     # which eval dataset this is measured on
    persona_segment: str = "all"  # or specific segment (e.g. "literacy_0_1")
    notes:           str = ""

@dataclass
class ConfidenceTarget:
    metric_name:     str     # e.g. "model_confidence_p50"
    threshold:       float   # minimum acceptable value
    action_below:    str     # what the product does if confidence falls below this

ACCURACY_TARGETS = [
    AccuracyTarget(
        metric_name="clause_extraction_accuracy",
        threshold=0.90,
        measurement_set="contract_eval_v2_500_samples",
        notes="Human-evaluated on random sample of 500 contracts from Q3 corpus",
    ),
    AccuracyTarget(
        metric_name="clause_extraction_accuracy",
        threshold=0.80,
        measurement_set="contract_eval_v2_500_samples",
        persona_segment="literacy_0_1",
        notes="Lower threshold for low-literacy persona: UI provides stronger guidance",
    ),
    AccuracyTarget(
        metric_name="hallucination_rate",
        threshold=0.03,   # max 3% of outputs contain fabricated facts
        measurement_set="contract_eval_v2_500_samples",
    ),
]

CONFIDENCE_TARGETS = [
    ConfidenceTarget(
        metric_name="model_confidence_per_finding",
        threshold=0.75,
        action_below="Show low-confidence badge; recommend human review",
    ),
    ConfidenceTarget(
        metric_name="model_confidence_per_finding",
        threshold=0.50,
        action_below="Block display; route finding to mandatory human review queue",
    ),
]
GenAI features depend on data at three points: training/fine-tuning data, retrieval/RAG data, and evaluation data. The PRD must specify all three with access status and readiness criteria.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class DataAccessStatus(str, Enum):
    CONFIRMED   = "confirmed"   # data exists and is accessible
    PENDING     = "pending"     # data exists but access not yet approved
    BLOCKED     = "blocked"     # access denied or data does not exist
    NOT_NEEDED  = "not_needed"

@dataclass
class DataRequirement:
    data_type:       str    # "training" | "retrieval" | "evaluation" | "fine_tuning"
    description:     str
    volume_estimate: str    # e.g. "~10,000 labelled examples"
    access_status:   DataAccessStatus
    owner:           str    # team or person responsible for access
    sprint1_ready:   bool   # True if needed in sprint 1

    def is_blocker(self) -> bool:
        return self.sprint1_ready and self.access_status == DataAccessStatus.BLOCKED

DATA_REQUIREMENTS = [
    DataRequirement(
        data_type="retrieval",
        description="Company contract corpus: all signed contracts from 2020–2024",
        volume_estimate="~4,200 PDF documents, ~1.2 GB",
        access_status=DataAccessStatus.CONFIRMED,
        owner="Legal Ops team",
        sprint1_ready=True,
    ),
    DataRequirement(
        data_type="evaluation",
        description="Labelled clause extraction dataset with human-verified ground truth",
        volume_estimate="500 contracts with 3,200 clause annotations",
        access_status=DataAccessStatus.PENDING,
        owner="QA engineer + external annotators",
        sprint1_ready=True,
    ),
    DataRequirement(
        data_type="fine_tuning",
        description="Domain-specific legal terminology corpus",
        volume_estimate="Not required in v1",
        access_status=DataAccessStatus.NOT_NEEDED,
        owner="",
        sprint1_ready=False,
    ),
]

blockers = [d for d in DATA_REQUIREMENTS if d.is_blocker()]
if blockers:
    print(f"SPRINT BLOCKERS: {[d.description for d in blockers]}")
The Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) section specifies exactly when humans must review, approve, or override AI output. This is distinct from the UX requirements section — it defines the

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class HITLTrigger(str, Enum):
    CONFIDENCE_BELOW  = "confidence_below_threshold"
    HARM_SEVERITY     = "harm_severity_threshold"
    FIRST_USE         = "first_use_by_persona"
    IRREVERSIBLE      = "irreversible_action"
    USER_REQUESTED    = "user_requests_review"
    ANOMALY_DETECTED  = "anomaly_detected"

@dataclass
class HITLRule:
    trigger:          HITLTrigger
    condition:        str       # specific condition value
    review_type:      str       # "approve_before_show" | "flag_for_async_review" | "notify_only"
    reviewer_role:    str       # who does the review
    sla_hours:        int = 24  # maximum time to complete review
    auto_escalate_after: int = 0  # 0 means no auto-escalation

HITL_RULES = [
    HITLRule(
        trigger=HITLTrigger.CONFIDENCE_BELOW,
        condition="model_confidence < 0.50",
        review_type="approve_before_show",
        reviewer_role="legal_reviewer",
        sla_hours=4,
    ),
    HITLRule(
        trigger=HITLTrigger.HARM_SEVERITY,
        condition="finding.harm_severity in ('significant', 'severe')",
        review_type="approve_before_show",
        reviewer_role="legal_reviewer",
        sla_hours=2,
        auto_escalate_after=2,  # escalate to senior if not reviewed in 2 hours
    ),
    HITLRule(
        trigger=HITLTrigger.IRREVERSIBLE,
        condition="action.type in ('send_to_counterparty', 'sign_contract')",
        review_type="approve_before_show",
        reviewer_role="contract_manager",
        sla_hours=1,
    ),
]

This section lists each input and output control with its trigger, action, and owner. It is the PRD-level version of the Guardrails row from the service blueprint, written for a non-technical audience.
Any document chunk sent to LLM
Route to HITL queue if score > 0.8
Disable approve button; require human gate
Strip PII patterns before file write

The Eval section defines the CI gates that block a release if quality falls below the thresholds defined in Section 2. It is the contract between the PRD and the engineering evaluation infrastructure.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class EvalGate:
    gate_id:     str
    description: str
    metric:      str
    threshold:   float
    eval_set:    str
    runs_in:     str    # "pr_ci" | "nightly" | "release"
    blocks:      str    # what it blocks if it fails

EVAL_GATES = [
    EvalGate(
        gate_id="EG-001",
        description="Clause extraction accuracy must meet target on eval set",
        metric="clause_extraction_accuracy",
        threshold=0.90,
        eval_set="contract_eval_v2_500_samples",
        runs_in="release",
        blocks="Release to production",
    ),
    EvalGate(
        gate_id="EG-002",
        description="Hallucination rate must not exceed 3% on eval set",
        metric="hallucination_rate",
        threshold=0.03,
        eval_set="contract_eval_v2_500_samples",
        runs_in="release",
        blocks="Release to production",
    ),
    EvalGate(
        gate_id="EG-003",
        description="Adversarial scenario suite must pass 100%",
        metric="adversarial_pass_rate",
        threshold=1.0,
        eval_set="adversarial_injection_suite_v1",
        runs_in="pr_ci",
        blocks="Pull request merge",
    ),
    EvalGate(
        gate_id="EG-004",
        description="P95 end-to-end latency must be within budget",
        metric="e2e_latency_p95_ms",
        threshold=8000,
        eval_set="latency_load_test_100rps",
        runs_in="release",
        blocks="Release to production",
    ),
]
Token cost and inference latency are first-class constraints for GenAI features. They must be specified in the PRD before implementation, not derived from the implementation after the fact.
The Observability Plan lists every metric that must be instrumented at launch. It is the mechanism that makes the Confidence & Accuracy Targets from Section 2 measurable in production.

The observability plan converts the PRD's quality targets into a monitoring commitment. If a metric is in Section 2 (Accuracy Targets) but not in Section 8 (Observability Plan), it will not be measured in production. Treat the observability plan as a checklist that must be implemented before the feature is declared done.
Each GenAI-specific section requires sign-off from a different stakeholder before sprint planning begins. Missing sign-offs are sprint-start blockers, not post-launch to-dos.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class PRDSection:
    name:       str
    sign_off_role: str
    is_blocker: bool    # True = sprint cannot start without sign-off
    status:     str = "pending"  # "pending" | "approved" | "blocked"

GENAI_PRD_SECTIONS = [
    PRDSection("AI Capability Brief",         "ML Engineer",           is_blocker=True),
    PRDSection("Confidence & Accuracy Targets","QA / ML Engineer",     is_blocker=True),
    PRDSection("Data Requirements",           "Data Engineer",          is_blocker=True),
    PRDSection("HITL Design",                 "PM + Designer",          is_blocker=True),
    PRDSection("Safety & Guardrail Spec",     "Security / Legal",       is_blocker=True),
    PRDSection("Explainability Requirements", "PM + Designer",          is_blocker=False),
    PRDSection("Eval & Quality Gates",        "QA / ML Engineer",       is_blocker=True),
    PRDSection("Cost & Latency Budget",       "Engineering Lead",       is_blocker=True),
    PRDSection("Observability Plan",          "Engineering Lead",       is_blocker=False),
    PRDSection("Degraded Mode Behaviour",     "PM + ML Engineer",       is_blocker=False),
]

def sprint_ready(sections: list[PRDSection]) -> bool:
    return all(
        s.status == "approved"
        for s in sections
        if s.is_blocker
    )

def blocking_sections(sections: list[PRDSection]) -> list[str]:
    return [s.name for s in sections
            if s.is_blocker and s.status != "approved"]

A GenAI PRD extends a standard product specification with eight sections that address the unique characteristics of probabilistic AI systems: capability feasibility, performance thresholds, data access, human oversight policy, safety controls, evaluation gates, cost constraints, and observability commitments.
The AI Capability Brief must include known limitations — omitting them does not eliminate them.
Accuracy targets must be per metric, per eval dataset, and per persona segment where they differ.
Data requirements cover three data types: retrieval, evaluation, and training — all with named owners and access status.
HITL rules specify the policy (when and by whom), not just the UX affordance (what the button looks like).
Each eval gate must specify which CI stage it runs in (PR, nightly, or release) and what it blocks.
Cost and latency budgets must appear in the PRD before implementation — deriving them from the implementation is too late.
If a quality metric appears in Section 2 (accuracy targets) but not in Section 8 (observability plan), it will not be measured in production.

The eight GenAI-specific PRD sections are the written contract between product, engineering, and safety teams. A sprint that begins without all blocker sections approved is starting without alignment on the most consequential design decisions in the feature.
